title: Collecting and using leachate from enclosed compost bins
subtitle: What the liquid from the bottom of a plastic compost bin is, and how to use it safely
excerpt: Enclosed plastic compost bins accumulate liquid at the base that drains through a tap or overflow hole. This leachate is not the same as compost tea: it is a nutrient-rich but often anaerobic liquid that needs diluting before use. This explains how to collect it, what it contains, how to dilute it for plant use, and when not to use it.
sourceNotes: Garden Organic composting factsheets on liquid feeds. WRAP Home Composting evidence review (2009). Haug RT, The Practical Handbook of Compost Engineering (1993) on leachate composition. Cornell Composting Science and Engineering resources.

glossaryTerms.definition: Liquid that has percolated through a compost mass, picking up dissolved organic matter, minerals, and sometimes pathogens. Compost leachate is darker and more anaerobic than aerated compost tea; it needs dilution before use as a plant feed.

A liquid plant feed made by steeping or aerating finished compost in water, distinct from leachate. Compost tea is deliberately aerated and inoculated to increase beneficial microorganism populations; leachate is a passive byproduct of decomposition.

content.text: Many plastic compost bins have a drainage tap or overflow at the base. The dark liquid that comes from this is

. The distinction matters because leachate from an enclosed bin can be anaerobic and may contain compounds harmful to plants if used neat. Diluted correctly, it is a useful liquid feed; used neat on seedlings, it can scorch roots or introduce pathogens.

Compost leachate is nutrient-rich: it typically contains nitrogen at 200 to 500 mg/litre, phosphate at 100 to 300 mg/litre, and potassium at 300 to 800 mg/litre. These are variable figures that depend heavily on what the bin contains and how wet it is. The liquid also picks up organic acids and in anaerobic conditions, sulphide compounds that give it an unpleasant smell. The nutrient content is useful; the anaerobic compounds are the issue for direct plant contact.

Place a small container under the drainage tap or overflow hole. Empty it when the container is around half full; left to overflow and pool on the ground, it creates an anaerobic wet patch that eventually kills the lawn beneath the bin. If your bin does not have a tap, lift the bin off its base once every few months and collect the pooled liquid from the base plate.
For established garden plants (shrubs, trees, established vegetables): dilute 10 parts water to 1 part leachate. Apply at soil level, not on foliage.
For seedlings: dilute 20:1 or avoid entirely if the leachate is dark and strong-smelling
For lawn use: 20:1 applied through a watering can during moist weather is safe and useful as a lawn feed

Do not use leachate from a bin that has recently had meat, fish, or pet waste added to it. Do not use it on salad crops, herbs, or any plant where the part you eat contacts the soil surface. Do not use it if it smells strongly of ammonia or sulphur: this indicates very fresh, immature decomposition. Let that leachate dilute in a bucket of rainwater for a week before applying.

Excess leachate indicates the bin is too wet. Add dry carbon material (wood chip, torn cardboard, shredded paper) in proportion to the wet food waste being added. A well-balanced bin with roughly equal parts green and brown material produces minimal leachate; most of the moisture is incorporated into the decomposing material and evaporates.
